sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Benzodiazepines (BZD) are sedative agents which act by potentiating the effects of the inhibitory neurotransmitter GABA within the central nervous system. They are one of the most common prescription drugs used in self-poisoning.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Symptoms usually manifest within 4-6 hours:
  - Ataxia
  - Drowsiness
  - Slurred Speech
  - Decreased GCS
  - Hypotension, bradycardia and hypothermia are possible with large ingestions

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why or how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm and mixed overdose
- For isolated benzodiazepine overdose, full recovery is generally expected with good supportive care
- Older patients, cardiorespiratory comorbidity, and co-ingestion of other CNS depressants increase the risk of complications
- Illicit or non-prescription benzodiazepines may be more potent, delayed, or contaminated
- Profound coma suggests co-ingestion of another CNS depressant, such as alcohol or opioids
- Check BGL in all patients with reduced conscious state or suspected overdose

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if the patient is awake and cooperative
- Position the patient to protect the airway
- Give oxygen if clinically indicated
- Perform repeated vital signs and neurological reassessment
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management if required
- Consider assisted ventilation if required
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG if clinically indicated
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider glucose if indicated
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Flumazenil should only be considered in highly selected cases of confirmed isolated benzodiazepine toxicity, after discussion with an appropriate senior clinician or toxicology service
- Do not use flumazenil when there is a risk of mixed overdose, seizure disorder, benzodiazepine dependence, or co-ingestion of pro-convulsant or dysrhythmogenic agents

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Examples of BZD’s include:**
  - Alprazolam (Xanax)
  - Clonazepam
  - Diazepam (Valium)
  - Midazolam
  - Nitrazepam
  - Oxazepam
  - Temazepam
  - Lorazepam (Ativan)
  - Flumazenil administration carries the risk or precipitating a toxidrome or a seizure in someone who has either taken a mixed overdose or has a history of epilepsy or chronic benzodiazepine use. If the patient has a seizure, you are now unable to give a benzodiazepine to terminate the seizure
  - Illicit non-prescription benzodiazepines are especially dangerous because the actual content and strength of the drug are often unknown and can vary widely.
Novel/designer benzodiazepines can be much more potent than prescription versions, increasing the risk of overdose, severe sedation, amnesia, and withdrawal. These types of benzodiazepines may contain other drugs or toxic substances, including synthetic opioids
  - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
